definition: A structural approach that lets you add responsibilities to individual objects dynamically by placing them inside lightweight wrapper objects that implement the same interface.
ruleOfThumb: Prefer wrapping objects to mix-in behaviour at runtime when inheritance would lead to many subclasses or rigid hierarchies.

citation.author: Erich Gamma, Richard Helm, Ralph Johnson, John Vlissides
citation.bookTitle: Design Patterns: Elements of Reusable Object-Oriented Software

questions.prompt: Look at the TypeScript snippet. Which statement best describes the technique used here?

options.text: Wrapping an object with another object that shares the same interface to extend behavior at runtime.
options.explanation: Correct. The Border class implements the same interface and holds a reference to a Component instance, delegating then adding behavior — a runtime wrapper around an object.

options.text: Creating a single concrete subclass to permanently extend functionality of the base class.
options.explanation: Incorrect. Subclassing would require extending SimpleWindow directly; here behavior is added by composition at runtime, not by creating a fixed subclass.

options.text: Exposing internal state from the component so callers can modify behaviour externally.
options.explanation: Incorrect. The code delegates and augments render output, it does not reveal or mutate the inner object's internal state for external modification.

options.text: Using a global helper function to transform the output of render calls.
options.explanation: Incorrect. A helper function would be a stateless transform. This example wraps the object and preserves identity and interface via composition.

questions.prompt: Which scenario makes this wrapping technique the most appropriate choice?

options.text: You need many orthogonal, combinable behaviors (e.g., logging, encrypting) that should be attachable at runtime.
options.explanation: Correct. Wrapping supports composing orthogonal responsibilities dynamically, avoiding explosion of subclasses for each combination.

options.text: You want to add a single, permanent field to every subtype so it exists on the concrete type itself.
options.explanation: Incorrect. If the addition must be a permanent field on every concrete class, subclassing or modifying the base type is more direct; wrappers add behavior without changing the wrapped object's concrete shape.

options.text: You need compile-time dispatch optimizations and no runtime overhead for behavior selection.
options.explanation: Incorrect. Wrapping introduces small runtime indirection; it is not aimed at zero-overhead static dispatch—it's chosen for flexibility, not micro-optimised call paths.

options.text: You want fewer runtime objects and prefer a single class with many conditional flags to select behavior.
options.explanation: Incorrect. Consolidating flags into one class increases complexity and violates single-responsibility; wrappers keep behaviors modular and decoupled.

questions.prompt: Which version would you pick for a library needing runtime composition of message-handling features (encryption, logging, retries) without combinatorial subclassing? Choose the better design and explain why.

options.text: Version A is better because concrete subclasses give clearer type identities and avoid the overhead of many small wrapper objects.
options.explanation: Incorrect. While subclassing yields distinct concrete types, it scales poorly: every combination needs a new subclass, leading to maintenance and duplication. The marginal runtime cost of a few thin wrappers is usually outweighed by their composability and reduced code duplication.

options.text: Version B is better because behavior wrappers compose at runtime, avoid combinatorial subclass explosion, and keep each responsibility isolated.
options.explanation: Correct. Wrappers implementing the same interface let you stack behaviors as needed (logging, encryption, retries) without producing a new class for every permutation. This preserves single-responsibility, is open for extension without modifying existing classes, and keeps tests focused on one concern per wrapper. The trade-offs are small delegation overhead and slightly more objects, which are acceptable in most application-level code.
